subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2, where you step through one of the most important doorways in all of mathematics: the doorway from arithmetic into algebra. Until now, most of your maths has used fixed numbers – 7 camels, 40 shillings, 12 dates. In this unit you will start using letters such as x, y and n to stand for numbers that can change or that you do not yet know. This single idea turns mathematics into a powerful language for describing patterns, writing rules, and solving problems that would be almost impossible with numbers alone.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning, so you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and copy every worked example onto paper, doing the steps yourself rather than only reading them. Algebra is a subject you learn through your pencil, not just your eyes. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra questions, explain a tricky step in a new way, or check your working line by line. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult.

One warning before we begin: algebra rewards care and neatness. A single lost minus sign or a term combined wrongly can change the whole answer. Do not rush. Write each step underneath the last, keep your equals signs lined up, and check your work by substituting a number back in. If you make a mistake, that is not failure – it is exactly the information you need to know what to practise next. Every mathematician, engineer and scientist you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same rules.
Understand a variable as a letter that stands for an unknown or changing number.
Identify the terms, coefficients and constants in an algebraic expression.
Write algebraic expressions from words, pictures and real-life situations.
Simplify expressions by collecting like terms, keeping signs correct.
Expand brackets using the distributive law, including negative multipliers.
Factorise expressions by taking out the highest common factor.
Substitute numbers, including negatives, into expressions and formulae.
Solve linear equations, including those with brackets and variables on both sides.

concepts.title: Variables, Terms and Expressions
concepts.explanation: A variable is a letter used to stand for a number. It might be a number you do not know yet, or a number that is allowed to change. When Amina writes n for the number of goats her family owns, that letter can be 12 one year and 15 the next – the letter stays the same, but the value it holds can change. This is the shift from arithmetic to algebra: instead of thinking about one fixed number, you think about a whole family of numbers at once. An algebraic expression is a group of numbers and variables joined by…
concepts.example: Every expression is built from terms. A term is a single piece of the expression, and terms are separated by + or − signs. In the expression 3x² + 2x − 7, there are three terms: 3x², 2x and −7. Notice that the minus…

concepts.title: Building Expressions from Words
concepts.explanation: A key algebra skill is turning ordinary language into an algebraic expression. Word problems are written in sentences, but to work with them you must translate them into symbols. The trick is to know which words point to which operations, and to choose a letter for the unknown quantity before you start. Here are the most common translations. More than and increased by mean add. Less than and decreased by mean subtract – but watch the order carefully. Times, product of and multiplied by mean multiply. Divided by…
concepts.example: 5 less than a number n → n − 5 (not 5 − n – order matters!)

concepts.title: Simplifying by Collecting Like Terms
concepts.explanation: Expressions are often longer than they need to be. Simplifying means writing an expression in its shortest, tidiest form without changing its value. The main tool is collecting like terms. Like terms are terms that have exactly the same variable part – the same letters raised to the same powers. Only like terms can be added or subtracted together. For example, 3x and 7x are like terms because both are just x to the first power, so 3x + 7x = 10x. Likewise 2x²y and −5x²y are like terms because both have x² and y.…
concepts.example: Why does collecting like terms work? It is really the distributive law used in reverse: 3x + 7x = (3 + 7)x = 10x. You are simply counting how many x's you have, just as 3 camels plus 7 camels makes 10 camels. You cannot…

concepts.title: Expanding Brackets
concepts.explanation: The distributive law is one of the most used rules in algebra. It says that for any numbers a, b and c: a(b + c) = ab + ac, and a(b − c) = ab − ac. In words, you multiply the term outside the bracket by every term inside the bracket. Removing brackets this way is called expanding. Start simple. To expand 3(x + 4), multiply the 3 by each term inside: 3 × x = 3x, and 3 × 4 = 12. So 3(x + 4) = 3x + 12. A very common mistake is to write 3(x + 4) = 3x + 4, forgetting to multiply the 4. The bracket means the 3 belongs…
concepts.example: Signs need extra care. To expand 5(2x − 3), we get 5 × 2x = 10x and 5 × (−3) = −15, so the answer is 10x − 15. When the multiplier is negative, every sign inside flips. To expand −2(3x − 4), we do −2 × 3x = −6x and −2 ×…

concepts.title: Factorising Expressions
concepts.explanation: Factorising is the reverse of expanding. Instead of removing brackets, you put them back. To factorise an expression, you find the highest common factor (HCF) of all the terms and take it outside a bracket. This is a powerful skill because a factorised expression often reveals structure that the expanded form hides. Look at 12x + 18. Both terms share a factor: the HCF of 12 and 18 is 6. Take out the 6, and ask what is left inside: 12x ÷ 6 = 2x, and 18 ÷ 6 = 3. So 12x + 18 = 6(2x + 3). You can always check a…
concepts.example: Try 15y − 10. The HCF of 15 and 10 is 5. Dividing gives 15y ÷ 5 = 3y and −10 ÷ 5 = −2, so 15y − 10 = 5(3y − 2). To *factorise completely* means to take out the largest possible common factor. Writing 15y − 10 = 5(3y −…

concepts.title: Formulae and Substitution
concepts.explanation: A formula is a rule, written with letters, that connects quantities. The formula for the area of a rectangle is A = lw, where A is the area, l the length and w the width. The letter that stands alone on one side is called the subject of the formula – here A is the subject. Formulae appear everywhere: in geometry, science, trade and daily life, because they capture a relationship once and let you use it again and again. Substitution means replacing each letter with a given number and then working out the value.…
concepts.example: Substituting negative numbers needs special care, and brackets are your best friend. To evaluate x² − 2x when x = −3, write (−3)² − 2(−3). Now (−3)² = 9 and −2(−3) = +6, so the value is 9 + 6 = 15. Without the brackets…

methods.title: How to solve a linear equation
methods.example: In the expression 6a²b + 2ab − 5, name the variables, the coefficients, and the constant.
steps: If there are brackets, expand them first.
Collect the variable terms on one side and the constants on the other.
Undo addition or subtraction, then undo multiplication or division.
Check by substituting your answer back into the original equation.

methods.title: How to substitute into a formula
methods.example: A taxi in Mombasa charges 5 shillings plus 2 shillings per kilometre. Write an expression for the cost of travelling k kilometres.
steps: Write each number in place of its letter, using brackets for negatives.
Work out powers first, then multiplication and division, then addition and subtraction.
State the answer with units if the formula has them.

methods.title: How to factorise
steps: Find the HCF of the numbers, and any letter common to every term.
Divide each term by the HCF to find what goes inside the bracket.
Write HCF(bracket), then expand to check you get the original.

methods.title: Expand and simplify
steps: Solution: Expand each bracket: 4(2x − 1) = 8x − 4, and −3(x + 2) = −3x − 6 (the minus multiplies both terms).
Now collect like terms: 8x − 3x = 5x, and −4 − 6 = −10.
The answer is 5x − 10.

methods.title: Factorise completely
steps: Solution: Find the HCF of 18, 12 and 6, which is 6.
Divide each term: 18a ÷ 6 = 3a, 12b ÷ 6 = 2b, and −6 ÷ 6 = −1.
So the answer is 6(3a + 2b − 1).
Check by expanding: 6(3a + 2b − 1) = 18a + 12b − 6.

methods.title: Substitute into a formula
methods.example: Using v = u + at, find v when u = 10, a = 3 and t = 4.
steps: Solution: Replace each letter: v = 10 + (3)(4).
Do the multiplication first: 3 × 4 = 12.
Following the order of operations keeps the answer correct.

workedExamples.title: Name the parts of an expression
workedExamples.prompt: In the expression 6a²b + 2ab − 5, name the variables, the coefficients, and the constant.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The variables are a and b. The coefficients are 6 (in 6a²b) and 2 (in 2ab). The constant term is −5, the piece with no variable. Remember to keep the minus sign attached to the 5.

workedExamples.title: Build an expression from words
workedExamples.prompt: A taxi in Mombasa charges 5 shillings plus 2 shillings per kilometre. Write an expression for the cost of travelling k kilometres.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The 5 shillings is fixed. The 2 shillings is charged once for each kilometre, so k kilometres cost 2k. The total is 5 + 2k. If k = 10, the cost is 5 + 2(10) = 25 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Collect like terms
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Group the x-terms: 6x − 2x = 4x. Group the y-terms: 4y + 3y = 7y. There are no other terms, so the answer is 4x + 7y. Keep the −2x sign attached as you gather the x group.

workedExamples.title: Expand and simplify
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Expand each bracket: 4(2x − 1) = 8x − 4, and −3(x + 2) = −3x − 6 (the minus multiplies both terms). Now collect like terms: 8x − 3x = 5x, and −4 − 6 = −10. The answer is 5x − 10.

workedExamples.title: Factorise completely
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the HCF of 18, 12 and 6, which is 6. Divide each term: 18a ÷ 6 = 3a, 12b ÷ 6 = 2b, and −6 ÷ 6 = −1. So the answer is 6(3a + 2b − 1). Check by expanding: 6(3a + 2b − 1) = 18a + 12b − 6.

workedExamples.title: Substitute into a formula
workedExamples.prompt: Using v = u + at, find v when u = 10, a = 3 and t = 4.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Replace each letter: v = 10 + (3)(4). Do the multiplication first: 3 × 4 = 12. Then v = 10 + 12 = 22. Following the order of operations keeps the answer correct.

workedExamples.title: Substitute a negative number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Write the substitution with brackets: (−3)² − 2(−3). Now (−3)² = 9 and −2(−3) = +6. So the value is 9 + 6 = 15. The brackets stop us from making a sign error.

workedExamples.title: Solve an equation with variables on both sides
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract 3x from both sides: 2x − 3 = 9. Add 3 to both sides: 2x = 12. Divide by 2: x = 6. Check: 5(6) − 3 = 27 and 3(6) + 9 = 27, so both sides match.

workedExamples.title: Solve an equation with a bracket
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Expand the bracket first: 4x − 8 = 20. Add 8 to both sides: 4x = 28. Divide by 4: x = 7. Check: 4(7 − 2) = 4 × 5 = 20. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Solve an inequality (with a flip)
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide both sides by −2. Because we divided by a negative number, we reverse the inequality sign: x < −3. Any number smaller than −3 satisfies the original inequality; you can test x = −4: −2(−4) = 8, which is indeed greater than 6.

workedExamples.title: Rearrange P = 2l + 2w to make w the subject
workedExamples.prompt: Rearrange P = 2l + 2w to make w the subject.

workedExamples.title: Solve with variables on both sides
workedExamples.prompt: Solve with variables on both sides: a) 2x + 5 = x + 12 b) 5x − 3 = 3x + 9.
workedExamples.solution: Use the section 3: challenge guidance and explain each step.

activities.title: Matchstick Patterns (Discover a Formula)
activities.materials: matchsticks, small sticks, or dry pasta.
steps: Make one square with 4 sticks. Add a second square joined to the first – how many sticks now? Add a third.
Record your results in a table: 1 square = 4, 2 squares = 7, 3 squares = 10, 4 squares = ___.
How many sticks does each new square add? Use this to write a formula for s sticks and n squares.
Check your formula: it should give 3n + 1. Test it for 5 squares and count to confirm.
This shows how a formula captures a growing pattern in one short rule.

activities.title: The Balance Scale (Solving Equations)
activities.materials: a simple balance (a ruler on a pencil), and small equal objects such as stones or beans, plus a hidden cup.
steps: Put a cup with an unknown number of beans plus 3 loose beans on one side, and 12 beans on the other, so it balances.
This models the equation x + 3 = 12. Remove 3 beans from BOTH sides to keep the balance.
The cup now balances against 9 beans, so x = 9. Open the cup and check.
Invent your own balance puzzles for a family member and let them solve for the hidden number.

activities.title: Algebra Bingo (Substitution Race)
steps: Each player draws a 3×3 grid and writes nine different numbers from −5 to 20 in the boxes.
One player calls an expression and a value, such as 2x + 1 with x = 4.
Both players work out the answer (here, 9) and cross it off if it is on their grid.
The first to cross off a full line wins. This trains fast, accurate substitution.

activities.title: Market Formula Investigation
activities.materials: a real or pretend market with priced goods (use stones as shillings).
steps: Choose an item, for example sambusas at 3 shillings each, and add a fixed 5-shilling delivery fee.
Write a formula for the total cost C of buying n sambusas: C = 3n + 5.
Use your formula to find the cost of 4, 7 and 10 sambusas without adding one at a time.
Now work backwards: if a friend paid 26 shillings, form the equation 3n + 5 = 26 and solve to find how many sambusas they bought.

activities.title: Expand-and-Factorise Card Match (Group Game)
activities.materials: paper cards, and two or more players.
steps: On some cards write expanded expressions (for example 12x + 18); on matching cards write the factorised form (6(2x + 3)).
Shuffle all cards and lay them face down in a grid.
Players take turns flipping two cards, trying to match an expanded form with its factorised partner.
Check each claimed match by expanding the bracket. Whoever finds the most matches wins.

activities.title: Inequality Number-Line Walk
activities.materials: chalk or a long strip of paper marked with a number line from −10 to 10.
steps: A caller shouts an inequality, such as x > 3, x ≤ −2, or −2x > 6 (solve to x < −3).
Walk to and stand on numbers that satisfy the inequality; a partner checks each one.
Use an open hand for a strict inequality (< or >) and a closed fist for ≤ or ≥ to show whether the end value is included.
Discuss why −2x > 6 requires flipping the sign when you divide by −2.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand variables and formulae best? What was the trickiest part – perhaps keeping signs correct, or flipping the inequality? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Collecting Like Terms Only combine terms with exactly the same variable part. 3x + 7x = 10x, but 2x + 3y cannot be combined. Keep each sign attached to its own term.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Distributive Law (Expanding) a(b + c) = ab + ac. Multiply the outside term by EVERY term inside. 3(x + 4) = 3x + 12. −2(3x − 4) = −6x + 8.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Factorising Take out the highest common factor (HCF) into a bracket. 12x + 18 = 6(2x + 3). Check by expanding.

rules.title: Balance Rule
rules.text: (Equations) Whatever you do to one side, do to the other. Undo operations one at a time until the variable is alone.

rules.title: Inequality Rule
rules.text: Solve like an equation, with ONE difference. If you multiply or divide by a negative number, reverse the sign. −2x > 6 gives x < −3.

terms: A letter standing for an unknown or changing number
A single part of an expression, separated by + or −
Terms with the same variable part
Terms joined by operations, no equals sign
A statement that two expressions are equal
A rule linking quantities with letters
commonMistakes: x and y differ, so leave as 2x + 3y
Dividing by a negative reverses: x < −3

questions.question: What does “Variable” mean?
options: A letter standing for an unknown or changing number
A single part of an expression, separated by + or −
questions.answer: A letter standing for an unknown or changing number
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Variable means A letter standing for an unknown or changing number.

questions.question: What does “Term” mean?
options: A single part of an expression, separated by + or −
Terms with the same variable part
questions.answer: A single part of an expression, separated by + or −
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Term means A single part of an expression, separated by + or −.

questions.question: What does “Coefficient” mean?
options: Terms with the same variable part
Terms joined by operations, no equals sign
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Coefficient means The number multiplying a variable.

questions.question: What does “Constant” mean?
options: Terms with the same variable part
Terms joined by operations, no equals sign
A statement that two expressions are equal
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Constant means A term with no variable.

questions.question: What does “Like terms” mean?
options: Terms with the same variable part
Terms joined by operations, no equals sign
A statement that two expressions are equal
A rule linking quantities with letters
questions.answer: Terms with the same variable part
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Like terms means Terms with the same variable part.

questions.question: What does “Expression” mean?
options: Terms joined by operations, no equals sign
A statement that two expressions are equal

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning, so you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and copy every worked example onto paper, doing the steps yourself rather than only reading them. Algebra is a subject you learn through your pencil, not just your eyes. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra questions, explain a tricky step in a new way, or check your working line by line. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult.

I can one warning before we begin: algebra rewards care and neatness. A single lost minus sign or a term combined wrongly can change the whole answer. Do not rush. Write each step underneath the last, keep your equals signs lined up, and check your work by substituting a number back in. If you make a mistake, that is not failure – it is exactly the information you need to know what to practise next. Every mathematician, engineer and scientist you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same rules.
I can understand a variable as a letter that stands for an unknown or changing number.
I can identify the terms, coefficients and constants in an algebraic expression.
I can write algebraic expressions from words, pictures and real-life situations.
I can simplify expressions by collecting like terms, keeping signs correct.
I can expand brackets using the distributive law, including negative multipliers.
I can factorise expressions by taking out the highest common factor.
